confidence_rationale: Foundational theoretical result: any finite game with a potential function has the Finite Improvement Property (all improvement paths terminate at Nash equilibria), making Nash equilibria reachable via decentralized best-response dynamics. If REE multiagent systems with other-cost-aversion can be cast as ordinal potential games, convergence to Nash equilibria -- and by extension to ethical attractors -- follows from this theorem. The confidence gap is the if: whether REE's counterfactual utility (MECH-127) preserves the ordinal potential structure is unproven.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Finite games with an ordinal potential function have the Finite Improvement Property: every sequence of unilateral better-reply moves terminates at a pure Nash equilibrium. Exact potential games are equivalent to congestion games. Fictitious play converges in any potential game. The potential function strictly increases along improvement paths, ensuring convergence without coordination.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-023 asks whether a REE multiagent system with other-cost-aversion primitives can be formally shown to converge to ethical attractors. Monderer and Shapley's framework provides the minimal sufficient condition: if the REE utility structure (including ARC-034's other-cost penalty and INV-028's harm internalization) admits an ordinal potential function, then best-response learning by REE agents converges to a Nash equilibrium. The ethical attractor is the Nash equilibrium of the potential game, which by the potential-maximizing property of stochastic stability (Young 1993) would be the one maximizing the potential function -- i.e., the one minimizing aggregate harm. This is the foundational mathematical gateway for Q-023.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The potential game framework requires that individual payoff differences from unilateral deviation have the same sign as potential function differences (ordinal) or the same value (exact). MECH-127 introduces counterfactual utility -- an agent's payoff depends on what would have happened without their action -- which creates interdependence that may break the ordinal potential condition. Whether the REE utility structure actually admits a potential function must be separately verified. The framework also assumes finite strategy spaces; REE agents operate over continuous action spaces in general.
mapping.source_context: Formal game theory; mathematical proof; applies to finite N-player games in strategic form

confidence_components.notes: Landmark mathematical paper with extremely high source quality. Mapping fidelity is the bottleneck: the potential game condition may not hold for REE's counterfactual utility structure (MECH-127). Transfer risk includes continuous-action extension and the gap between Nash equilibrium convergence and 'ethical attractor' convergence -- these are not identical.
